speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 17, 1995. The survivor is Freda Weiss. And this is tape three. Please continue, Mrs. Weiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, then they came in one morning, early in the morning. And they said everybody should stand up. They wanted to look us over to see whom they can pick to send to work in the labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we did. And they picked me between 100 maybe-- no, maybe thousands woman. But 100 from our camp. And others was from different barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: They picked me. And the other who was remaining there, they put a number on the hand. I don't have the number because they picked me and they shipped me to labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said we shouldn't take along nothing. Because we all have the same uniform dresses, long gray dresses with wooden shoes. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing on the body, no slips or even no panties. No underwear, nothing. Just the gray uniform dress. They picked me and mine cousins, [? Suri, ?] [? Reizi-- ?] they were twins, [? Suri ?] and [? Reizi, ?] two girls, mine age. And we are-- we were cousins. And the two other girls from the same hometown. And we went together, and five in the row. We went to the train. Which the train waited for us somewhere far-- I wouldn't remember, exactly, the place. And we were going with the train to the Gelsenkirchen, the city of Gelsenkirchen. That's in Ruhrgebiet. The city was tremendous bombarded from the-- or from the enemies or from the Allies, whatever. For us, it was enemies all the time. Because we suffered. Even it was the Allies, even it was the United States, which we-- I love it. But then it wasn't, for me, an ally. We suffered a lot. And we had to pick-- to clean up the city. The bombarded houses, everything from the floor. They made a row, one by one. And we picked up a stone or whatever, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rubble.

speaker: interviewee
text: --dirt, and gave it to the second. The second gave it to the third, and so on. They put it in a pile. So we cleaned up. When this was finished, they put us in another street, in another street. And this was for three months. For three months, every early in the morning, they put us in the streets to pick up the-- the-- the misery, what it was there. The food was nothing. Still that empty soup with a piece of bread. Black bread that were-- bread was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1944, still.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: In April, we went. It was already, May, June, July.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In July, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was very hot. And they gave us to sleep, those cells, the-- made from material they put up on the fields?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was maybe 10 tents. And it was a few hundred girls in a tent. No beds, on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were satisfied. What can we do? People was crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: People was [INAUDIBLE]. People were sick, a lot of people. They shouldn't say that they are sick. It wasn't allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was watching over you? Who was in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a woman SS, a man SS. The SS. Also they picked from our-- from our people they picked out a few. They should be in control of the others. But most, the woman [? SSes ?] were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people that were chosen amongst yourselves--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some not too good. Because they were miserable. And they said to the people whom they picked, if you're not going to do a good job, you know what you going to-- what we going to do with you. They warned them. And they were scared. So they didn't want to do favors for nobody. In fact, they hit, they punched. And they hit some of our own, because they-- they wanted to live. And they were scared for the-- for the [? SSes. ?] And-- and one night, being in the-- in the tents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] across the street from the tent was a gasoline station. And it was bombarded from one of the [? war ?] [? airs. ?] And the flames were so terrible that it came straight to the tents. And we got scared for the flame, they going to burn us. So we run out, everybody, few thousand women, run out from the tents. And everybody in the same uniform and the wooden shoes. We thought it was the Allies, who thought that we are German soldiers. Because from far, from up there, maybe they didn't see that we are women. Maybe they wouldn't do it. They bombarded us. Grenades. They-- it came so many grenades. I was running with those four girls, I was the fifth, by the hand. We run by the hand, everybody, like that. And we saw just that heads goes on one side, legs go on the other side, from the same person. Hands goes, and they-- they were split. They were-- they were killed, in pieces. And I was in the side. And an SS was in a bunker. In, you know, just a bunker as-- for them. Not for us. And he put out his hands. He reach-- reached my hand. And he schlepped me in his bunker. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [DRINKS] I couldn't talk to him. He just said, shh, don't say to nobody. But he was nice. He didn't do a thing to me, no. He didn't touch me even. Just he schlepped me and I shouldn't be killed. Sure enough, those four girls got killed. I was the only-- God was all  over with me. If I would tell you how many times. One time I ran. I ran. I wanted to kill myself. I just couldn't take it anymore. I ran to the wires, electrical wires. And an SS saw me running. He ran after me. He ran much faster than I did. He catched me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He hit me. He said, you dummy. Why you going to kill yourself? You want to kill yourself? Let me kill you. I didn't answer him, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, go back to your place. So I was saved, true. Even he was an SS, and he saved me from the wire, electrical wire. And in no time, [? that, ?] in Gelsenkirchen, of the flames, the bombardment, and the grenades killed 275 people. Young girls, two sisters, from my hometown, [? Aggi ?] and [? Gisella. ?] Both two sisters, one 16 and one 18. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when it was finished, the bombardment, they said, louse. Louse. Go out. Go out. We had to pick together all the bodies, all the pieces from those women and put in a pile, and match.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we burned them. We had to do that. The remaining women from that. We were all very sick about it. We were so sick. And then they knew. They knew we cannot do, anymore, the work there. Because we were very-- We said, you can kill us. But we cannot go to work. What we went through and what we saw-- and no food, and no drinks, and no news from the parents, where they are. And no news from our loved ones. We don't know nothing from the whole world, what's going on. Nothing, just like animals. Even animals they wouldn't kill like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they saw it. In the good for themselves, they thought they take us away from it. We was three months there, July, August, September, October. October, they said, so we go somewhere else. They took us, again, but with trains, to Soemmerda. Soemmerda is a city munition factories. Municion-- munition? Ammunition.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition factories. And they put us there. Underground, they had it. And we went there. And they gave everybody work there, in the ammunition. I made the coal, that, what you kill the people. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The bullet?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bullet. I made the bullet. They showed me a few times. And then I knew, already, how to do it, and  I made the bullet. And we was there from the morning to the night, making those bullets. Thousands and thousands. Very, extremely big place. Underground, not-- people should know that it was hidden. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And almost to the year, almost-- no, it was about two months before. We were there October, November, December.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. That was Germany still. Then they said the Allies was already very close to occupy that Soemmerda. And when they find-- when they knew, they didn't want us to stay alive and to be occupied. They took us away from there. March, march, again, five in the row they put us. And they start walking with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: What shall I tell you? We walked for weeks, day and night, day and night. Sometime it was a [INAUDIBLE] alarm bombardment in there, then we fell down to the ground. And we relaxed then. What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, already, in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: November? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: November.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very cold, raining cold. And nothing, no sweater, no. Some girls took their-- their blankets. And they knew to sew. They arranged, somehow from the SS, to give them a needle with-- with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thread. And they made, from the blankets, a jacket. So some of them had jackets from blankets. But not everybody could sew. And not everybody had the opportunity to have connection with the [? SSes, ?] that they should help out with a needle and thread.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think they had this connection with the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? Some SS men, there was beautiful girls. Gorgeous. One of mine girlfriends, her name was Rachela Berkowitz from Maramures, Sighet. Elie Wiesel was very friendly with them. I used to go there. And-- and I took lessons, sewing lessons. But they know to sew very good. And one of the [? SSes ?] loved her. Not loved-- I mean, she didn't go with him. I don't think so. But they liked her a lot. She organized that for herself, for somebody else, somebody else. But she couldn't do it a lot. Because it wasn't-- it wasn't right for her to do it. Because then he is going to stop the whole thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked. We walked, day and night. You know what we did?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very dry in the mouth, and very thirsty and hungry. When the SS went to eat, they had pork and soups. And they had everything. But we didn't have nothing. When they went to eat, some of the girls went out from the road. They said, I don't care, let it kill me. From the-- they find a little water on the floor, walking. They went with the tongue and they licked-- licked out the water from the floor. They took grass. And they put it in their mouth. And they eat. This was our food. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Sometime there was  SS that they threw a [NON-ENGLISH], a bun. They threw it just for-- just to show us that we should cry, that we should throw a [NON-ENGLISH]. They eat meat.

speaker: interviewee
text: They throw us the bones. What you think? We schlep the bones? We took them and we-- we eat them, whatever came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- and then after we were, already, four weeks walking, they wanted to hid us in the-- not barracks. It was where the hay was kept from agriculture people. There was a houses--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barn-- barns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barns, correct. They put us in, in the barns. [SIGHS] That was heaven. There was hay. We could lay down. We could relax. And then that man, it was Czechoslovakian already, close, brought us potatoes, baked potatoes, and gave to everybody two, three potatoes. Cry-- they-- they-- they cried like babies, those people, seeing us. We were-- we were skin and bone, skin and bone. We couldn't talk. And-- and-- and the pus was all over and running. And a lot of people, blood was running. Because they hit themselves. And-- and that was [? Purim, ?] [? Purim, ?] those-- those agriculture people, they had, there, a lot of fields. They brought us-- and they gave us something to eat. And they brought water. And they gave us to drink, water. And after one night being there, relaxed, again, five in  the row. And we go again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was, already, close to Sudetenland. Kraslice. The name of the city is Kraslice, Sudentenland, close. So many they shot to death. They couldn't walk anymore. And they stopped walking. And they said, kill me. A lot of women begged the [? SSes, ?] kill me. [LAUGHS] They didn't have to beg them. They did it gladly. And they-- they-- they-- and they left them there. We went further. And we saw-- we saw there, [? that stays ?] my friend, there-- there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to the end walking in a field was a very big alarm, a bombardment. Ooh, with the rain and-- and everybody was falling to the ground, and the [? SSes, ?] also, everybody. And I knew that I'm going to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I couldn't walk anymore. But listen to that, what God does. I think-- I still-- how can I believe? But I still believe in God, what he [? then ?] [? did ?] me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get up. And I looked around. And I saw everybody's on the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw, in-- in the right-hand side, a little house, very far away. I start running. I didn't stay in the ground. My cousin [? Suri, ?] [? Frimet-- ?] my Jewish name is [? Frimet-- ?] where are you running? Where you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't answer. I run. I run in, in that little house. Big, a big house. It was a big house there, [? near ?] [? the ?] [? big ?] [? house. ?] But the Germans-- it was-- still was Germany-- they evacuated from there. Because the bombardment was very great, very terrible, bad. So they were afraid to stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they left the house, their houses. Nobody was there. I run in, in that big house. I covered myself with that dirt. I fall asleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few hours later, I just heard that the command is walking. And they singing, [GERMAN]. Hallelujah! Hallelujah! (SINGING) [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew that. Because all the time when we was walking, they said, Freda, where is your mouth? Why don't you sing? I had a very strong voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I hear them going, I thought, oh, thank God, thank God. Now I'm liberated. Even I don't have nobody. Even I'm alone. But I'm liberated from the Nazis. Maybe other ones are going to come find me. But in the moment, I was happy that they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was singing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was singing that? Who did you  hear sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: The girls were singing. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The girls had to sing. If not, they-- they were beaten. They couldn't sing, they were so weak. But they must. They must sing. The Nazi-- the SS said, you sing along when you walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and I hear that. And I knew that song. So I was very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that they are leaving the place. And they went. And I was there. In middle of the night after they left, I-- the bombardment was still very heavy. And the little room where I was sitting in was moving from the bombs. Moving from place to place. And I say, I'm going to get killed. And I didn't want to die. I didn't want to die. I went through so much, now I want to live. I went and I was-- I had a handkerchief over my head. I don't know how I did with that handkerchief.

speaker: interviewee
text: I find it in that there. I took that handkerchief, put it on my head. And I said, I'm going. Where to go? I went out from little house. I looked right. I look left. I look in straight. Where shall I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't see no people. And it's still dark. I thought, a Jew goes to the right, to myself, because I was religious. And I went to the right. And God-- God did it to me. I walked and walked and walked maybe an hour, falling down, getting up, falling down, and getting up, and walked. All of a sudden, I came in, in a city where music is playing, German music, singing, dancing, and lights, a lot of lights. I went in there. I thought to myself, or I live or I die, I can't go on like that. Going, walking like that in front of me from far, I see three SS men you know, with a sign on the-- on their clothes. I thought to myself, now is the end of my life. They going to see me, recognize me, that I'm a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] means a refugee from the Holocaust. And they going to kill me, I thought to myself. But I said, no. I went straight to them in the face. And I said, [GERMAN]. Dear, your honor, or-- I gave them a title. I fell asleep in the commanding [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I would love to go after them to be with them, together. But I don't know where. Could you help me? Could you show me where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They looked at me. They looked at me. And they say, [GERMAN]. They didn't do a thing to me. They said, go. Go ahead! Go away! Good. They go this way. I go that way. And I go. Go another hour walking, another hour. No food, hungry, thirsty. Almost dead. I don't know how I walked, really. I see a little narrow street under-- up to the woods. I thought, oh, I'm going there. And I saw three lights, dim, dim lights. I thought, oh, there's lights, people live there. I'm going there. To myself, I thought. And I go up. As I come close up the high hill, dogs comes. And rips my dress, mine-- mine only dress, what I have. And puts me to the floor. Doesn't bite me. Doesn't give me even not one scratch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't that [INAUDIBLE] there? [NON-ENGLISH] means God. Wasn't he there? Three Shepherds, dogs, German dogs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they ripped mine dress and takes mine shoes away. And-- and-- and doesn't scratch me, even. And they run away. When I was already on the floor, laying there. They thought I'm dead. The dogs run away. I got up, naked, rip here, here, there. Almost naked, I walk. I don't go to the first little house, which was closer. I don't go to the second. I went to the last one, to the third. It was already early in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knock on the door. Somebody says, [GERMAN]? Who is there? I says, [GERMAN]. Come out. You will see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The door opens. And I don't say a word. Just I fall to her feet. I kiss her, the feet. I kiss her, the hands. I say, [GERMAN]. Dear lady, please, or you help me or you kill me. Please, do me a  favor. If you cannot help me, which I understand you are  afraid, kill me. Don't let me suffer. I can't-- I can't do it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, [GERMAN]. Dear child, poor child. Why? She said, like that, why? I says, I don't know. Just please, help me or kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She grabbed my hand. She pulled me in. She pulled me down to the basement, a German woman, democratic German woman. And she tells me that, mine dear child, four weeks ago, my husband was killed in front of mine eyes and my children's eyes. The Gestapo came, that he should put on the SS clothes. And he refused. He said, I don't want to live if you-- if I have to kill.

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed him in front. Right away, they killed him. So why shall I not help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are animals. They are not people. They are not human being. They are-- they're just no good. And she put me-- she took off of the pieces of the dress. She put me in the bathtub. She made me a bath. Then she bathed me. She cleaned me up. She brought me out a housecoat and slippers. She put it on, on me. And she took me up in the kitchen, to her kitchen. And in the kitchen there were her mother, old lady, and a sister. And two other girls, Russian girls, non-Jewish.
